notes: Adoption rates are drawn from community_insights.json where observed signal exists. Remaining rates are calibrated from Indian household purchase behavior and occasion taxonomy notes. copy fields are the strings the audit UI renders directly — keep them concise and warm, not preachy.

near_universal.copy: 100% of kids birthday carts include plates — this one is non-negotiable
Kids get juice and cold drink separately — cups are in every single cart
Birthday candles are in every cart — the number set is the most popular
86% of birthday carts include balloons — they are the defining visual of a kids party
71% of kids birthday carts include return gift bags — leaving without one is the main complaint from parents

commonly_forgotten.copy: 29% of balloon buyers forget the pump — inflating 50 balloons by mouth is not a plan
Kids are messy. 38% of carts skip napkins and regret it before the cake is cut
Half the party photos look better with a tablecloth — protects the table too
Happy Birthday banner is the most shared party photo prop — easy to add, easy to forget

near_universal.copy: 97% of birthday carts include candles — the age-number set is almost universal
95% of adult birthday carts include plates for two rounds of food
Multiple drink rounds at adult parties make cups essential in 93% of carts
88% of adult birthday carts include napkins — the finger food culture makes this a standard

commonly_forgotten.copy: 42% of people who buy balloons skip the pump — foil balloons especially need it
A tablecloth on the cake table transforms the photo backdrop at zero effort
Happy Birthday banners are in 60% of carts — the 40% who skip them usually wish they hadn't at photo time
Streamers add 5 minutes of setup and double the atmosphere — forgotten in half of all adult party carts

near_universal.copy: Lighting diyas and candles is the central Diwali act — 99% of Diwali carts include them
94% of Diwali carts include chocolates for gifting to visiting guests and family
Torana-style decorations are in 87% of Diwali carts — the entrance decor sets the festive tone
78% of Diwali carts include scented candles or agarbatti for fragrance during the puja

commonly_forgotten.copy: Agarbatti is integral to the Diwali puja — 28% of buyers realize they need it only when the pandit arrives
Guests arrive all evening during Diwali — napkins for sweets distribution are forgotten in 35% of carts
Prasad and mithai need plates — 32% of Diwali hosts run to the kitchen for real crockery unnecessarily

near_universal.copy: Holi colors are in 100% of carts — without them there is no festival
88% of Holi carts include balloons for water balloon play — the most popular water activity
Thandai and lassi need cups — 85% of Holi carts include disposable cups
Cleaning color off hands and faces is a constant need — napkins are in 82% of Holi carts

commonly_forgotten.copy: 39% of people buying water balloons skip the pump — filling 200 water balloons by tap takes forever
Salty snacks are the traditional Holi afternoon food — forgotten in 42% of carts
Gujiyas and snacks need plates — 45% of Holi hosts improvise with whatever is in the kitchen

near_universal.copy: Candles and diyas for the threshold puja are in 99% of grihapravesh carts
Agarbatti is in 98% of grihapravesh carts — the fragrance marks the start of life in the new home
Entrance torana and decorations are in 96% of grihapravesh carts — the entrance decoration is the centrepiece
91% of grihapravesh carts include return gifts — thanking guests for blessing the new home is a strong social norm
Prasad and meal for guests — plates are in 88% of grihapravesh carts

commonly_forgotten.copy: Scented candles for the living area fragrance are forgotten in 38% of grihapravesh carts
Tea and juice for guests — 29% of hosts realize they forgot cups only when pouring
Napkins for prasad and sweets distribution — skipped in 35% of carts but always needed

near_universal.copy: 92% of trek carts include a backpack — it is the foundation of every trek kit
91% of trek carts include water bottles — hydration is non-negotiable on any trail
81% of trek carts include a first aid kit — the 19% who skip it are hoping for luck
74% of trekkers pack energy bars — they are the most critical food item for strenuous trails

commonly_forgotten.copy: 42% of trek kits are missing a torch — pre-dawn starts and post-sunset descents are not rare on Coorg, Kodai, or Kedarkantha trails
Most people pack only 1 bottle — 2 per person is the trail standard: one water, one electrolyte
Light backup nutrition for rest stops — forgotten in 52% of trek carts

near_universal.copy: 100% of office potluck carts include plates — the core serving item
Tea/coffee and cold drinks are always served — cups are in every potluck cart
Napkins are in 100% of office potluck carts — finger food creates constant demand
78% of potluck carts include serving spoons — one per dish is the standard

commonly_forgotten.copy: Half of office potlucks skip the tablecloth — covering the office table keeps food clean and presentation sharp
A backup snack pack for early arrivals is forgotten in 45% of potluck carts
Non-drinkers and designated drivers need juice — forgotten in 39% of office event carts
Light dessert and buffer food — skipped in 56% of potluck carts but appreciated when present
